meta.title: Mercedes GLE250d GLE300d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d engine — OM651 and OM654. W166 and V167 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM651 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,500 supply only; the OM654 costs £3,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600. Turbocharger replacement on the OM651 can cost £1,500–£3,500 if caught early. Timing chain replacement is an engine‑out job. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the generation. The OM651 (W166, 2015–2019) is rated ★★½☆☆ — in the heavy GLE, the engine works harder and engine bay temperatures run hotter. 72% of turbo failures occur between 75,000–95,000 miles, and timing chain stretch is a well‑documented weakness. The OM654 (V167, 2019–present) is rated ★★★☆☆ — more modern but has valvetrain wear, AdBlue system faults, and a coolant pump recall.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651 turbocharger failure (72% between 75k–95k miles), timing chain stretch, oil cooler seal leaks, piezo injector failure, and oil leaks from the turbo and main seal. OM654 valvetrain wear (camshafts and tappets), rocker arm bearing failure, AdBlue system faults (NOx sensors, AdBlue injector), and a coolant pump vacuum circuit recall.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well‑kept V167 GLE300d worth £25,000–£40,000, a £3,500–£8,500 recon is a manageable 9–34% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well‑maintained example. For a W166 GLE250d worth £8,000–£15,000, a £4,500 recon is more marginal.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLE250d uses OM651 (W166 2015–2019); the GLE300d uses OM654 (V167 2019–present). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651's timing chain, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain and tensioner replacement (£1,500–£3,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£2,500–£5,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. The chain sits on the transmission side of the engine, so it's an engine‑out job, but it's far cheaper than a new engine.

eraMap.title: The GLE250d / GLE300d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The OM651 in the heavy GLE — timing chain stretch, turbo failure at 80k miles, and oil cooler leaks are the defining weaknesses.
The modern OM654 — valvetrain wear, AdBlue system faults, and potential coolant pump leaks are the main watchpoints.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLE250d / GLE300d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLE diesel engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Timing chain replacement on the OM651 is an engine‑out job, significantly increasing labour costs.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLE250d / GLE300d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace turbo; 72% fail between 75k–95k miles — catch it early
If caught early; engine‑out job but cheaper than replacement
Labour‑intensive but straightforward; catch it before oil loss causes damage
Replace failed injectors with Delphi or Bosch units
Cylinder head work required; catch it before camshaft failure
Replace NOx sensors, pump or injector; don't let countdown expire
Check recall status — free dealer repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLE Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes (more frequent than manufacturer recommendation)
* Post‑2019 OM654 — generally more refined than the OM651
* Evidence of turbocharger replacement on OM651 cars (especially 75k–95k mile examples)
* No AdBlue system issues (V167) or evidence of NOx sensor replacement
avoidIf: * OM651 with turbo noise or power loss — 72% fail between 75k–95k miles
* Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history (OM651) — the single most important check
* Cold‑start misfire or ticking (OM654) — potential valvetrain wear
* AdBlue countdown active (V167) — potential system failure
* Poor service history on any generation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLE250d / GLE300d is a premium mid‑size SUV that offers refinement, space and excellent fuel economy. But the engine choice determines your ownership experience. The OM651 (W166, 2015–2019) is worked harder in the heavy GLE than in lighter models — engine bay temperatures run hotter, accelerating wear. Turbo failure between 75,000–95,000 miles is a well‑documented weakness, along with timing chain stretch, oil cooler leaks, and injector failure. The OM654 (V167, 2019–present) is more modern but has valvetrain wear, AdBlue system faults, and a coolant pump recall. Buy on documented history, and if you're looking at an OM651, be prepared for potential turbo and chain work. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz GLE250d / GLE300d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any loss of power, whistling, or grinding? 72% of OM651 turbos fail between 75k–95k miles
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling noise on startup (OM651 timing chain warning) or misfire/judder (OM654 valvetrain wear)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM651 for W166 2015–2019, OM654 for V167 2019–present) — see the Era Map above
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car or in the engine bay — OM651 oil cooler leaks and turbo oil leaks are common
* AdBlue System — Any AdBlue warnings? Countdown active? NOx sensor or pump failure (V167)
* Coolant Pump Recall — Has the OM654 coolant pump/vacuum circuit recall been completed?
* Fuel Pump ECU — Check for water ingress in the spare wheel well — can damage the fuel pump control unit
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved turbo, timing, injector, or emissions issues?
